cataloging_subject.label: The Towneley Plays (HM 1 manuscript compilation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous manuscript-specific compilation represented in the pinned U-M/OTA XML and uniquely witnessed by Huntington Library HM 1: thirty-one principal biblical dramatic units plus a later, non-dramatic fragmentary Judas monologue traditionally numbered as item 32. It is not a modern selection, a securely unified sequential-performance cycle, an abstract authorial work, the later Epp edition's reorganized 27-unit sequence plus appendix, the physical manuscript, the 1897/1966 source edition, or the electronic encoding.

catalog_summary.title: The Towneley Plays (HM 1 manuscript compilation)
catalog_summary.author: Multiple anonymous playwrights
catalog_summary.editor: George England and Alfred W. Pollard (1897/1966 source edition); University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive encoding (1993)
catalog_summary.date: Constituent plays of varied earlier dates; HM 1 cataloged 1490-1510, while Epp argues likely 1550s; electronic encoding 1993
catalog_summary.region: Northern England; exact origin and the nature of the Wakefield association remain unresolved

notes: The qualifier makes the manuscript-compilation boundary explicit and avoids treating all units as a securely established coherent Wakefield performance cycle.

assertion.notes: Retained only as a historically influential label; current evidence does not securely establish its implied unity or performance attribution.

notes: Do not use this label as evidence that the compilation was performed sequentially by Wakefield guilds.
Exact current Huntington catalog label; its word 'cycle' is not adopted here as a structural conclusion.

agents.name: Wakefield Master
notes: A disputed critical construct for an unidentified author associated especially with the distinctive thirteen-line stanza; it is not a documented historical identity.

scope.description: The thirty-one principal dramatic units, which are of varied origins and do not establish one author

notes: Part-level borrowing, adaptation, and revision complicate any simple one-author account.

scope.description: Assembly and shaping of the manuscript collection rather than authorship of every constituent play
Five plays conventionally associated with the distinctive thirteen-line stanza and the disputed Wakefield Master

assertion.notes: The label remains useful for discussing a stylistic grouping but does not identify a known person; Cain and Abel is sometimes added on other grounds and is not silently included here.

scope.description: Later fragmentary Judas monologue added by a hand other than the principal scribe

date_statements.display: Varied earlier dates; most constituent plays predate the surviving manuscript

scope.description: The thirty-one principal play texts rather than the later Judas addition or the date of their compilation

notes: No single composition date is normalized across works of different origin and revision history.

scope.description: Institutional catalog dating of the surviving HM 1 compilation

assertion.notes: This is an attributed catalog assertion and conflicts with Epp's later dating.

scope.description: Current scholarly argument for production of the main manuscript compilation

assertion.notes: Epp argues from script, initials, and religious-historical context; the exact date remains unresolved.

places.label: Northern England
notes: A broad cultural region only; the exact place of manuscript production and of each constituent play is unresolved.

places.label: Wakefield, West Yorkshire
notes: The name appears in the manuscript and local allusions exist, but the relationship between manuscript, texts, and Wakefield performance is disputed.
This broad association does not flatten the constituent plays' varied origins.

assertion.notes: Wakefield appears on the first extant page and with Noah, and Cain and Abel contains a local allusion, but the collection's precise origin, purpose, and performance relationship remain unknown.

scope.description: Intermittent rubrics, invocations, liturgical phrases, and brief formulae embedded across the compilation rather than sustained Latin text

form_statements.description: All thirty-two top-level XML units are represented in verse. The logical count of 12,293 rows recombines 117 final and 11 medial continuation fragments rather than treating every L element as a separate intellectual line.

assertion.notes: The 2018 Epp edition has a different editorial lineation of 14,385 lines; neither count is silently treated as universally canonical.

genre_statements.label: Manuscript compilation
notes: Primary whole-scope genre for a disparate manuscript collection; it avoids forcing the units into a coherent performance cycle.

subject_statements.label: Passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus

summaries.value: The Towneley Plays preserve an anonymous, manuscript-specific assemblage of Middle English biblical drama moving broadly from Creation through Judgment, with comic shepherd material, Nativity and Passion sequences, Resurrection appearances, and related episodes. The collection is heterogeneous: constituent plays have varied origins and production requirements, several draw on the York dramatic tradition, and current scholarship no longer treats the manuscript as secure evidence for a single sequential Wakefield guild cycle. A later fragmentary Judas monologue, copied by another hand, is traditionally counted as item 32.

The pinned encoding has thirty-two top-level DIV1 units: thirty-one principal dramatic units and The Hanging of Judas. Its order follows the traditional manuscript/1897 sequence. Epp's later edition reorganizes that material as twenty-seven numbered units plus a Judas appendix, combining Isaac with Jacob, reconstructing an Advent sequence, separating Annunciation from Joseph's Trouble, moving Lazarus, and making other editorial changes; that later arrangement is documented but does not replace the corpus boundary.

This record describes the complete intellectual/content boundary encoded in Towneley.xml, not every play ever associated with Wakefield, every lacuna conjectured for HM 1, the physical codex as an object, or later editorial reconstructions. The surviving HM 1 is the unique manuscript witness of this compilation, but some constituent plays have parallels or antecedents in other traditions, especially York; uniqueness of the compilation is not uniqueness of every episode or version.

The XML has 32 DIV1, 32 HEAD, 12,421 L, and 396 PB elements. Recombining 106 initial, 117 final, and 11 medial PART fragments produces 12,293 represented logical verse-line rows; sixteen standalone unnumbered L rows and the First Shepherds' numbering gap are retained rather than normalized away. Epp's current edition totals 14,385 lines because it makes different editorial decisions about internal rhyme and structure. Content structure is complete relative to the pinned XML even though HM 1 itself is materially lacunose.

extent.description: Thirty-two top-level verse units in the pinned XML: thirty-one principal dramatic units plus a later fragmentary Judas monologue. The 12,293 logical row count recombines continuation fragments from 12,421 raw L elements. It is an encoding-relative representation metric, not a claim that Epp's alternative 14,385-line editorial lineation is wrong.

content_parts.label: The Creation
content_parts.summary: God creates heaven, angels, the world, and humankind; Lucifer's rebellion and the fall frame the need for redemption.

content_parts.label: The Killing of Abel
content_parts.summary: Cain and Abel offer sacrifices; Cain murders Abel, is cursed, and resolves to hide from judgment.
notes: The pinned XML has 475 L elements; 473 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Noah and the Ark
content_parts.summary: Noah obeys God's command to build the ark while his wife resists; the family survives the Flood and gives thanks.
notes: The pinned XML has 569 L elements; 558 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Abraham
content_parts.summary: Abraham prepares to sacrifice Isaac at God's command until an angel stays his hand; the surviving text is incomplete at its end.
notes: The pinned XML has 312 L elements; 286 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Isaac
content_parts.summary: The aged Isaac is deceived into blessing Jacob instead of Esau, and Jacob is sent away for safety.
notes: The pinned XML has 71 L elements; 70 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Jacob
content_parts.summary: Jacob journeys toward Mesopotamia, receives divine assurance, and is reconciled with his brother.
notes: The pinned XML has 145 L elements; 142 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Prophets
content_parts.summary: A sequence of prophets announces the coming incarnation and salvation; the manuscript unit is unfinished.

content_parts.label: Pharaoh
content_parts.summary: Moses confronts Pharaoh, the Israelites depart Egypt, and Pharaoh's host is destroyed in the sea.
notes: The pinned XML has 435 L elements; 432 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Caesar Augustus
content_parts.summary: Caesar's imperial authority and decree for enrollment prepare the narrative setting for Christ's birth.

content_parts.label: The Aunnunciation
content_parts.summary: Gabriel announces Christ's conception to Mary; Joseph's distress and reassurance form the latter portion of the unit.

content_parts.label: The Salutation of Elizabeth
content_parts.summary: Mary visits Elizabeth, who recognizes the blessed pregnancy, and the women exchange praise and blessing.

content_parts.label: The First Shepherds' Play
content_parts.summary: Shepherds lament hardship, hear the angelic announcement, and visit the Christ child with humble gifts.
notes: The pinned XML has 523 L elements; 503 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.
The source numbering skips 501; the line total is based on represented logical rows, not a presumed canonical sequence.

content_parts.label: The Second Shepherds' Play
content_parts.summary: The Mak sheep-stealing farce gives way to the angelic announcement and the shepherds' visit to the Christ child.
notes: The pinned XML has 768 L elements; 754 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Offering of the Magi
content_parts.summary: The Magi follow the star, encounter Herod, offer gifts to Christ, and avoid returning to Herod.

content_parts.label: The Flight Into Egypt
content_parts.summary: Warned by an angel, Joseph leads Mary and the child toward Egypt to escape Herod's violence.

content_parts.label: Herod the Great
content_parts.summary: Herod responds to the Magi's evasion by ordering the massacre of the Innocents.
notes: The pinned XML has 517 L elements; 513 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Purification of Mary
content_parts.summary: Mary presents Jesus at the Temple, where Simeon recognizes the promised child; the unit is incomplete at its end.

content_parts.label: The Play of the Doctors
content_parts.summary: The young Jesus debates learned doctors in the Temple and is reunited with Mary and Joseph; the beginning is missing.

content_parts.label: John the Baptist
content_parts.summary: John preaches repentance, baptizes his hearers, and directs them toward Christ.

content_parts.label: The Conspiracy
content_parts.summary: Religious authorities conspire against Jesus, Judas bargains to betray him, and Jesus is arrested.
notes: The pinned XML has 756 L elements; 755 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Buffeting
content_parts.summary: Jesus is taken before Annas and Caiaphas, mocked and struck, while Peter's denial unfolds.
notes: The pinned XML has 479 L elements; 450 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Scourging
content_parts.summary: Jesus is examined by Pilate, condemned, scourged, mocked, and led onward to execution.
notes: The pinned XML has 417 L elements; 416 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Crucifixion
content_parts.summary: Soldiers raise Jesus on the cross; his death, lamentation, and the theological meaning of the Passion are dramatized.
notes: The pinned XML has 672 L elements; 666 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Talents
content_parts.summary: Soldiers cast lots for Christ's garments and quarrel over their shares after the Crucifixion.

content_parts.label: The Deliverance of Souls
content_parts.summary: Christ descends to hell, defeats its powers, and releases Adam and the other righteous souls.

content_parts.label: The Resurrection of the Lord
content_parts.summary: Guards confront the empty tomb, Christ rises, and the news and appearances of the Resurrection spread.
notes: The pinned XML has 635 L elements; 634 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: The Pilgrims
content_parts.summary: Two disciples encounter the risen Christ on the road to Emmaus and recognize him in fellowship.

content_parts.label: Thomas of India
content_parts.summary: Thomas doubts and then touches Christ's wounds before receiving instruction for his apostolic mission.

content_parts.label: The Lord's Ascension
content_parts.summary: Christ commissions the disciples and ascends; the surviving unit breaks off before its original ending.

content_parts.label: The Judgement
content_parts.summary: At the Last Judgment, the dead rise, accusations are heard, and the saved and damned receive their destinies; its beginning is lost.
notes: The pinned XML has 628 L elements; 622 logical verse-line rows remain after recombining PART continuation fragments.

content_parts.label: Lazarus
content_parts.summary: Jesus comes to Bethany, consoles Martha and Mary, and raises Lazarus from the dead.

content_parts.label: The Hanging of Judas
content_parts.summary: A later, non-dramatic and fragmentary Judas monologue recounts accumulated sins and betrayal before suicide; its ending is missing.
notes: Traditionally numbered as play 32, this is treated by Epp as a later appendix rather than a dramatic play by the main scribe.

related_works.title: The York Corpus Christi plays
notes: Epp identifies substantial York-tradition borrowing or adaptation in Pharaoh, Doctors, Harrowing of Hell, Resurrection, Judgment, probably Dice/Talents, one Magi stanza, and much of Scourging.
No direct edge to the surviving York Register is asserted: some Towneley readings may predate that register, and the exact exemplars remain unknown.

evidence.summary: Supplies the exact thirty-two-unit boundary, headings, electronic title and source statement, encoded text, editorial notes, and historical availability statement.

evidence.citation: Element, PART-fragment, node, page-milestone, and line-number audit of the pinned Towneley XML.
evidence.locator: All BODY/DIV1/HEAD/L/PB elements and NODE/N/PART attributes
evidence.summary: Counts 32 DIV1, 32 HEAD, 12,421 L, 396 PB, 106 PART=I, 117 PART=F, and 11 PART=M elements. Recombining continuation fragments yields 12,293 logical line rows; per-part raw and logical counts and printed-page spans were verified.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The Towneley plays.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current public item, title, anonymous authorship label, 1966 source-publication statement, OTA identifier, thirty-two-item contents, and current No Copyright - United States designation.

evidence.citation: Towneley lineage manifest evidence for Huntington Library HM 1.
evidence.summary: Supports the unique surviving manuscript-compilation boundary, current institutional date, foliation, physical description, lacunae, late Judas hand, order, provenance, restrictions, and selected-image coverage.

evidence.citation: Towneley lineage manifest evidence for Garrett P. J. Epp's current TEAMS/METS edition and introduction.
evidence.summary: Supports the disparate-compilation model, the lack of secure evidence for one coherent Wakefield cycle, Wakefield uncertainty, likely mid-sixteenth-century dating, York-tradition relationships, alternative 27-unit-plus-appendix analysis, folio ranges, lineation, and source-edition history.

evidence.citation: Towneley lineage manifest evidence for the complete 1897 England-Pollard EETS edition.
evidence.summary: Supports the traditional thirty-two-unit print order, pp. 1-396 locators, title-page responsibilities, stated manuscript consultation, and publication history.

evidence.citation: Towneley lineage manifest evidence for the complete 1836 Surtees Society edition.
evidence.summary: Supports the first complete publication date, complete text, manuscript access, and proof comparison against HM 1.

open_questions.question: When was the main HM 1 compilation produced?
next_steps: Preserve the Huntington 1490-1510 catalog assignment and Epp's likely-1550s argument as attributed alternatives; seek updated codicological or paleographical analysis before normalizing one date.

open_questions.question: What is the compilation's precise relationship to Wakefield, performance, and devotional use?
next_steps: Retain Wakefield as an association rather than a composition or performance fact; review new documentary, linguistic, and codicological scholarship before changing the compilation model.
notes: Current evidence does not support treating all units as a coherent guild cycle for sequential performance; Epp suggests devotional reading under Mary Tudor as a possibility, not a proven purpose.

open_questions.question: Which York-tradition exemplars underlie the part-specific borrowings?
next_steps: Keep York relationships part-scoped and non-physical until textual scholarship identifies specific exemplars; do not equate the surviving York Register with a proven direct source.

open_questions.question: How much of the 1897 EETS text derives directly from HM 1 rather than the 1836 Surtees edition?
next_steps: Preserve both the 1897 editors' new-transcript/proof-correction claim and Epp's finding that 1836 was substantially the base; a systematic variant audit would be needed to quantify dependence.

open_questions.question: Did the 1966 OUP reprint make any substantive or paratextual changes to the 1897 EETS edition?
next_steps: Inspect a physical or licensed complete copy of the 1966 reprint and compare title matter, pagination, text, appendix, and corrections against the cached 1897 volume.

open_questions.question: Is a complete public digital facsimile of all surviving HM 1 leaves available?
next_steps: Recheck the Huntington catalog, exact shelfmark search, compound object, and IIIF manifest; meanwhile use the public 60-canvas selected object, request the restricted manuscript, order reproductions through Aeon, or consult the complete 1976 printed facsimile onsite.
notes: The official Huntington object explicitly says the manuscript was only partially scanned and publishes 60 non-contiguous canvases.
Content parts follow the exact traditional/XML order; Epp's later reordering and recombination are editorial analysis, not a replacement cataloging hierarchy.
The 31 principal play units and the later Judas fragment are counted separately so that traditional item number 32 remains discoverable without misclassifying it as a main-scribe dramatic play.
Shared printed boundary pages are intentional: adjacent units may begin and end on the same 1897 page.
The Huntington catalog's 'unique manuscript' claim is interpreted as uniqueness of this compilation, not as proof that every constituent play lacks parallel versions.
The XML header's unqualified 1460 creation date is retained as a legacy source assertion only; it is not normalized as the date of either the heterogeneous constituent plays or HM 1.
No place-of-composition statement is made for Wakefield, and no normalized compilation date is preferred across the institutional and scholarly conflict.
